figures.caption: FIG. 4. Steady-state shape of the drops for various contact angles θ. The drop shape corresponds to contour of color-field φ = 1 2 . Solid red and dashed blue lines correspond to wetting boundary condition of Fakhari et al. [20] and present scheme, respectively. Measured contact angles computed via Eq. (12), and shown in Fig. (6), highlight difference between the two wetting boundary conditions.

FIG. 5. Spurious (parasitic) fluid velocity currents distribution for contact angles θ = 30° and θ = 150° obtained with the present wetting boundary condition. Magnitude of maximum fluid velocities for these two cases are 5.74× 10−7 and 7× 10−7, respectively.

FIG. 6. Difference of measured and set contact angle θm−θ as a function of set contact angle θset. As the set contact angle deviates from the neutral one, the present wetting boundary scheme is more accurate than that of Fakhari et al. [20].

FIG. 7. Error in contact angle as a function of interface width W . For a given contact angle θ, increasing W ∆x effectively decreases magnitude of the small parameter |ε| = 4∆x W | cos θ|. For a moderate grid resolution W ∆x = 4, the present wetting scheme performs better than the scheme of Fakhari et al. [20].
FIG. 8. Schematic of a stationary drop sitting on a solid cylinder of radius R under negligible gravity. The contact angle is θ, and the height of the apex of the droplet at steady state is hmax.

FIG. 9. Scaled height of the drop hmax R along y axis as a function of contact angle θ. As the contact angle θ deviates from the neutral contact angle, the present wetting scheme produces better accuracy, albeit slightly.

FIG. 10. A cylindrical particle of radius R is placed at a flat liquid-gas interface such that center of the particle is on the interface. Periodic and no-slip boundary conditions are applied in the horizontal x and vertical y directions, respectively. Depending on the contact angle θ, the contact line will adjust its position on the particle while conserving mass of both the fluid phases.

FIG. 11. Vertical displacement h∗ of the liquid-gas interface from the initial position at the periodic boundary x = 0 as a function of contact angle θ. h∗ is obtained by numerical solution of Eq. (13).

FIG. 12. Schematic of a drop of initial radius Rd falling on a fixed cylinder of radius Rs, due to gravity. The height of the evolving film h is obtained from simulations, and compared against analytical scaling law [38, 39].

FIG. 13. Contours of φ = 0.5 showing the drop shapes obtained using the proposed scheme (blue dashed line) and Fakhari et al.’s [20] (solid red line) scheme for Bo = 2.2, and θ = 40° and 170° at various time-instants. t∗g = t √ g/2R is time scaled with gravitational time scale.

FIG. 14. Scaled height h of the drop film on the cylinder surface as a function of scaled time t. Here, h = h/2R, where h is the measured film height in the simulations, and t = (t − ti)Ui/2R, where ti is the time of impact and Ui is the impact velocity. Both, Ui and ti, are estimated by relating the initial potential energy of the drop with the final kinetic energy at the instant of impact.
TABLE I. MLUPS comparison between FP32 and FP64 for 50, 000 iterations, averaged over 10 runs
